verdicts.reason: absint() returns a non-negative integer. The preg_replace uses it as a quantifier in the regex pattern. No user input reaches the function parameters directly; the variable is an integer used in a masking utility function. Not RCE.

REQUEST_URI is processed through sanitize_text_field/parse_url/preg_replace to extract a REST route path. The preg_replace uses a hardcoded pattern with no /e modifier. This is URL parsing logic, not command execution. Not RCE.
Same flow as rce-002. The preg_replace extracts the REST route prefix from a URL path. Hardcoded regex pattern, no /e modifier. Not RCE.
wp_safe_redirect() validates the redirect target against the site's allowed hosts list, preventing open redirect. The redirect_to parameter is properly constrained to same-origin URLs.

$wpdb->prepare() IS used with %s placeholder. The scanner incorrectly flagged 'prepare not used' but the source expression itself contains the prepare() call. The login username is safely parameterized.
absint() returns an integer. The value is used in a sprintf with %d format specifier. No XSS is possible from an integer value in a format string that expects a decimal number.

The $setting from get_option() is immediately cast to boolean (1 or 0) via ($setting ? 1 : 0). Only the integer result is used in the output. The $name variable uses htmlspecialchars(). Not exploitable XSS.
Same pattern as xss-002. The $setting is cast to boolean integer. Role names come from WordPress core $wp_roles object, not user input. htmlspecialchars() is applied to the display name.
$wpdb->prepare() IS used with %s placeholder. Same as sqli-001 - the scanner misidentified the prepare() call within the source expression. Safely parameterized.

parse_str() output goes to a local variable $posted_settings, not extract()'d into scope. Only specific keys (tfa_algorithm_type, tfa_enable_tfa, etc.) are read from it. The handler requires authentication (wp_ajax_ hook) and nonce verification. No variable injection risk.

verdicts.title: Missing esc_url() on Trusted Device IP in href Attribute
verdicts.reason: The IP address from $device['ip'] is placed in an href attribute without esc_url(). However, the IP is sourced from $_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR'] (TCP-layer, not attacker-controlled in standard deployments). The display text uses htmlspecialchars() correctly. Exploitability requires the IP source to be attacker-controlled (misconfigured reverse proxy). Code defect but low practical risk.

Admin-only code path (manage_options required). The $per_page comes from a PHP constant via absint(), which returns a non-negative integer. The $orderby uses sanitize_value_by_array() whitelist + sanitize_sql_orderby(). No injection possible.
filter_var with FILTER_VALIDATE_IP rejects anything that is not a valid IP address. The validated IPs are then used in .htaccess rules. Admin-only code path. Not RCE.
Admin-only code path (manage_options + nonce). The $entries value is wrapped in absint() which returns a non-negative integer. DELETE WHERE id='<integer>' is safe from injection.

Admin-only code path. The event_type filter uses sanitize_text_field() + esc_sql() inside single-quoted SQL string literals. The filter key names are hardcoded in the code, not from user input. sanitize_value_by_array() whitelists orderby. Not exploitable.
Same code path as sqli-005, different sink (get_results vs get_var). Same protections apply. Admin-only, esc_sql in quoted context, whitelisted orderby.
Admin-only. $per_page from absint() of a constant (integer). Same protections as sqli-005/006 for the WHERE clause. Not exploitable.
Admin-only (manage_options menu page). orderby goes through sanitize_text_field -> esc_sql -> sanitize_value_by_array (whitelist) -> sanitize_sql_orderby. Four layers of protection. Not exploitable.
Same code path as sqli-008 with LIMIT/OFFSET variant. Same four-layer protection. Admin-only. Not exploitable.
Admin-only (manage_options). Search term uses sanitize_text_field + esc_sql in quoted SQL context. Orderby whitelisted. Not exploitable.
Same as sqli-010, different sink. Admin-only. Not exploitable.
Same as sqli-010/011, LIMIT/OFFSET variant. Admin-only. Not exploitable.
Admin-only. orderby through sanitize_text_field -> sanitize_value_by_array whitelist. Not exploitable.
Admin-only. $entries via absint() (integer). DELETE WHERE id='<integer>' is safe.
Admin-only (manage_options). orderby through sanitize_text_field -> esc_sql -> sanitize_value_by_array whitelist. Not exploitable.
Same as sqli-015, LIMIT/OFFSET variant. Admin-only. Not exploitable.

The $m variable is (int) cast. It is used with selected() function and in comparison logic. The printf output uses esc_attr() and esc_html() on all interpolated values. No XSS possible from an integer.
wp_safe_redirect() validates against allowed hosts. The HTTP_HOST is sanitized. Pre-5.2 legacy code. No open redirect.
wp_safe_redirect() constrains to same-origin. Pre-5.2 legacy code. Not exploitable.
wp_safe_redirect() constrains to same-origin. Pre-5.2 legacy code. Not exploitable.

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated 2FA Status Disclosure via simbatfa-init-otp AJAX
verdicts.reason: The wp_ajax_nopriv_simbatfa-init-otp handler accepts a username via POST and returns whether that user has 2FA enabled (status:true/false). No nonce, no rate limiting. An attacker can enumerate all accounts and identify those without 2FA protection, then target them with credential stuffing. This is a design choice for the login form JS, but the lack of any anti-automation makes it an enumeration oracle.

verdicts.title: IP-Based Security Controls Bypassable When Proxy Headers Configured
verdicts.reason: When administrator configures IP retrieval to use HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR or similar headers (options 1-6), any unauthenticated user can spoof their IP by sending a crafted header. This bypasses login lockout, IP blacklists, and all IP-based firewall rules. Default fresh installs use REMOTE_ADDR (safe), so this is configuration-dependent. No trusted-proxy validation is performed when header-based methods are selected.
